coredata.dc:title: Enhancing moisture-electric power generation through in situ incorporation of carbon dots into polyelectrolyte membrane
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Zeyong
Li, Ning
Chang, Qing
Xue, Chaorui
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: The electric generation performance of traditional polyelectrolyte membrane with moisture usually fall far behind expectations due to its limited hydrophilic groups and poor directional transport capacity of protons. To this end, we introduce the suspension of carbon dots (CDs) prepared by selective oxidation etching of coal pitch with the mixture of formic acid and H2O2 under ambient temperature into the aqueous solution of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and poly(4-styrensulfonic acid) (PSS) to fabricate a novel hybrid membrane. This membrane (1.00 cm2) continuously delivers a voltage output of 0.83 V with a short-circuit current of 5.20 μA under proper moist conditions over a prolonged period, outperforming the power generation performance of the pristine membrane and many current moisture-enabled electric generation (MEG) devices. Moreover, CDs endow the polymer membrane with more superior mechanical properties, in favor of processing and scale integration for higher power output. With excess hydrophilic groups provided by as-prepared CDs’ suspension, the denser hydrogen-bond network can be formed in the membrane and facilitates water adsorption and the formation of a higher proton concentration gradient. CDs-induced bridging interactions between PSS chains pave a fast directional transport pathway for moveable protons, resulting in a remarkable power output. Accordingly, this work offers a facile and economic method for remoulding traditional polyelectrolyte membrane to conduct highly efficient MEG for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coal-based carbon dot
Moisture-enabled electric generation
Polymer membrane
Moveable hydrated ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and performance characterization of moist-electric generator
The roles of CDs in MEG

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the fabrication process for a MEG. (b) Voltage output of the membranes containing different amounts of CDs. (c) Current output of the membranes containing different amounts of CDs. (d) Stress–strain curves of the PSS, PSS-PVA and PSS-PVA@CDs. (e) Voltage output of the PSS-PVA and PSS-PVA@CDs powered by breath moisture. Insert: photograph showing a breathing electric harvesting mask. (f) Voltage output of the PSS-PVA and PSS-PVA@CDs powered by surface skin moisture. Insert: photograph for electric generation powered by surface skin moisture.

(a) MEG performance comparisons of the reported various materials with PSS-PVA@CDs. (b) Current output of parallel MEGs. The inset is the schematic of a parallel connection. (c) Voltage output of serial MEGs. The inset is the schematic of a serial connection. (d) Photograph of the voltage output of three serial MEGs. (e) An 8-digit electronic calculator powered by serial MEGs. (f) Voltage output stability of MEG in 20 days.

(a) Voltage and current output of PSS-PVA, PSS-PVA@FA and PSS-PVA@CDs; (b) Schematic illustration for testing EIS and the corresponding results; (c) Water contact angles of PSS-PVA, PSS-PVA@FA and PSS-PVA@CDs; (d) Adsorbed water curves of PSS-PVA, PSS-PVA@FA and PSS-PVA@CDs with time; (e) Adsorbed water rates of PSS-PVA, PSS-PVA@FA and PSS-PVA@CDs; (f) Voltage outputs of PSS-PVA, PSS-PVA@FA and PSS-PVA@CDs in 15 min.

(a) Zeta potentials of the mixed solution containing different amount of CDs; (b and c) PL spectra of PSS-PVA, PSS-PVA@FA and PSS-PVA@CDs and the corresponding photographs under 365 nm ultraviolet light; (d and e) Schematic illustration of molecular structures for PSS-PVA and PSS-PVA@CDs; (f and g) Mechanism of the MEG process for PSS-PVA and PSS-PVA@CDs in moisture.

(a) Voltage output of PSS-PVA@CDs with two surface electrodes without drilled holes as illustrated in the scheme in the inset; (b) Voltage output of PSS-PVA@CDs before and after sealing the holes on the electrode; (c) Voltage output of PSS-PVA@CDs with two surface electrodes drilled holes as illustrated in the scheme in the inset; (d) EIS spectra of PSS-PVA@CDs vs. time exposed in moisture; (e) H+ concentrations of PSS-PVA, PSS-PVA@FA and PSS-PVA@CDs immersed in 6 mL IPA and 1 mL water; (f) Voltage and current output of PSS-PVA@CDs in response to different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Enhancing moisture-electric power generation through in situ incorporation of carbon dots into polyelectrolyte membrane

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electric generation performance of traditional polyelectrolyte membrane with moisture usually fall far behind expectations due to its limited hydrophilic groups and poor directional transport capacity of protons. To this end, we introduce the suspension of carbon dots (CDs) prepared by selective oxidation etching of coal pitch with the mixture of formic acid and H2O2 under ambient temperature into the aqueous solution of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and poly(4-styrensulfonic acid) (PSS) to fabricate a novel hybrid membrane. This membrane (1.00 cm2) continuously delivers a voltage output of 0.83 V with a short-circuit current of 5.20 μA under proper moist conditions over a prolonged period, outperforming the power generation performance of the pristine membrane and many current moisture-enabled electric generation (MEG) devices. Moreover, CDs endow the polymer membrane with more superior mechanical properties, in favor of processing and scale integration for higher power output. With excess hydrophilic groups provided by as-prepared CDs’ suspension, the denser hydrogen-bond network can be formed in the membrane and facilitates water adsorption and the formation of a higher proton concentration gradient. CDs-induced bridging interactions between PSS chains pave a fast directional transport pathway for moveable protons, resulting in a remarkable power output. Accordingly, this work offers a facile and economic method for remoulding traditional polyelectrolyte membrane to conduct highly efficient MEG for practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Moisture-enabled electric generation (MEG) becomes rising as a promising method toward clean energy harvesting and conversion without the burden of much loss of accompanied other energy during the conversion [1–7]. The mechanism of MEG has been attributed to directional proton (that is, free hydrated ion) transports under the proton concentration gradient induced by ambient moisture diffusion in polyelectrolyte membrane [8–11]. As thus, many proton conductive polymers with hydroxyl, sulfonic acid and carboxylic groups such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), poly(4-styrensulfonic acid) (PSS), polyacrylic acid (PAA), Nafion, hydroxyethyl cellulose, natural polysaccharides and etc., can realize electric generation via the release of movable protons under moisture diffusion [12–14]. However, the output power of their made nanogenerators remains quite low, limiting their practical application so far [15,16]. Although new materials and microstructures with high porosity and high water affinity have been used to improve radical gradients and facilitate moisture diffusion, the large electric output still faces the challenge of miniaturization and large-scale integration of MEG units owing to their poor flexibility and processability [17,18].

Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical and non-toxic fluorescent nanoparticles that consist of nanometer-sized carbon cores surrounded by numerous heteroatom groups (e.g. oxygen, sulfur and phosphorus-related groups) with moveable positively charged ions [19–21]. Due to their intrinsic ultra-small sizes, they are endowed with unique properties relevant to edge effects and quantum-confinements and allow them bridge the gap between the molecular and the nanoscale world [22,23]. Accordingly, CDs can be prone to tightly combining with a variety of organic molecules with the help of reactive groups on their surfaces [24]. The CDs embedded into organic polymers could not only boost the adsorption of moisture through their surface groups but also provide transportation tunnels of protons by creating spaces at the interfaces of them and polymers. As a result, the introduction of CDs into polyelectrolyte membranes offers an exciting possibility to promote MEG’s output performance without incurring their mechanical property deterioration.

We recently have developed large-scale preparation of CDs by selective oxidation etching of coal pitch with the mixture of formic acid and H2O2 (10:1 V/V) under ambient temperature [19]. The resulting CDs were decorated with abundant carboxylic acid and alcohol groups and uniformly dispersed in residual reaction solution. For large-scale application of these low-cost CDs, as-prepared suspension of CDs without any further purification was directly introduced into the reaction solution of PSS and PVA to prepare a novel hybrid membrane of MEG. This method not only enabled CDs to incorporate into membrane by in situ interaction with polymer molecules but also took full advantage of the residual formic acids to increase carboxylic groups in membranes, in favor of energy and environment sustainability.

In this work, we reported a highly efficient moist-electric generator fabricated by CDs-incorporated hybrid membrane mentioned above. It generated a considerable electric output, remarkably superior to those of CDs-free membrane and other reported MEG devices based on various materials. By simply connecting MEGs in series or in parallel, the resultant device viably drove commercial electronics, showing its high potential in portable and self-powered systems. Moreover, we unveiled the roles of CDs played in polyelectrolyte membrane for promoting MEG’s output performance. Therefore, this work paved a novel pathway for remoulding traditional polyelectrolyte membrane to conduct moist-electric energy conversion for practical applications.

Sample membrane preparation: The suspension of CDs was prepared by our reported method [19]. Typically, 100 mg coal pitch powders were reacted with the mixture of 15 mL of formic acid (88 v/v%) and 1.50 mL of H2O2 (30 v/v%) for 26 h under room temperature. By simply centrifugal separation, the CDs’ suspension with a concentration of 2.00 ± 0.20 g·L−1 was obtained. Taking different volumes of CDs’ suspension was put into 6.50 mL of the hot aqueous solution (96 °C) containing PSS (Mw ∼ 70,000, 30 wt% in H2O, 1 mL) and PVA (Mw ∼ 75,000, 60 mg). After stirring for 20 min, the resultant mixture was poured into a plastic petri dish and dried for 4 h in oven set at 40 °C, obtaining the membrane with a thickness of 0.10 cm labeled as PSS-PVA@CDs. For comparison, the pristine membrane (PSS-PVA) was prepared following the same processes as for PSS-PVA@CDs except for no addition of CDs’ suspension. Moreover, another control sample was further achieved by replacing CDs’ suspension with the equivalent amount of the mixed solution of formic acid and H2O2 (10:1 V/V) and named as PSS-PVA@FA.

Electrode preparation and signal collection: To prepare an MEG as illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the obtained membrane was cut into pieces (1 × 1 cm2) and then sandwiched between the two electrodes made by graphite paper. The edges were sealed with hot glue. Holes with a diameter of 0.10 cm were drilled on the top electrode as moisture ingress. A small piece of insulation foam was pasted on each of electrodes to act as the clip connector for testing. The electric signals of MEGs were collected in real-time through a General Purpose Source Meter (Keithley Model 2400) under moisture. The voltage output test was set current into 0nA and the current output test was set voltage into 0 mV. To avoid any inference from the static electricity, all the samples were short circuited before testing.

Characterization methods: The mechanic tensile tests were conducted by a universal testing machine (SANS CMT 6104) with a stretching rate of 10 mm·min−1. Fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Hitachi F4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected by using a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer from 4000 to 1000 cm−1. X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) data of all samples were recorded by employing a Kratos AXIS 165 mutitechnique electron spectrometer with an Al Kα X-ray source for further determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations. Water contact angles were measured with an OCA 15 Plus Contact Angle Meter (Eastern-Dataphy Inc., China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and performance characterization of moist-electric generator
ce:section.ce:para: The proper volume of as-prepared suspension of CDs was mixed with the aqueous solution of PSS and PVA to directly cast into a free-standing membrane labeled as PSS-PVA@CDs (Fig. S1). For testing MEG, as-prepared sample membrane (1 × 1 cm2) was sandwiched between two graphite paper electrodes (Fig. 1a and Fig. S2). Thereinto, one side electrode was processed with holes to enable moisture to freely access the sample membrane.

Fig. 1b provided the equilibrium output voltages of sample membranes adding different amounts of CDs suspension after stabilization. It illustrates that the introduction of CDs can increase electric generation of the membrane within unchanged moisture feeding with the relative humidity (RH) of 90%. Note that the optimal amount of CDs suspension added in the PSS-PVA hybrid is 0.10 mL for as-prepared CDs. It generated a maximum V oc value of 0.83 V, reaching 127% times higher than the sample of PSS-PVA without adding CDs. If adding more CDs will significantly reduce the quantity of PSS-PVA matrix for the same thickness film, the number of moveable protons released along with the water molecule diffusion could be also reduced, causing the decrease in electric signals as displayed in Fig. 1b. More impressively, the output current of PSS-PVA@CDs is 200% times larger than that of PSS-PVA (Fig. 1c), indicating that the incorporation of CDs in the membrane are conducive to rapid transport of free hydrated ions. For a 1 × 1 cm2 membrane of PSS-PVA@CDs, the effective output power achieves 0.60 μW when the external load resistance placed in the circuit is about 1.00 MΩ (Fig. S3). Besides the RH (Fig. S4), our experimental results revealed that the thickness and the area of membrane used could cause the differences of interior water gradient and thereby had an influence on the performance of MEG (Figs. S5 and S6).

To examine the effect of CDs on the mechanical properties of as-prepared membrane, Fig. 1d presented the typical stress–strain curves of PSS, PSS-PVA and PSS-PVA@CDs. PSS-PVA@CDs exerted excellent mechanical properties and achieved the tensile strength of above 14.00 MPa along with fracture strain of close to 40%. In comparison, PSS merely had a tensile strength of 8.00 MPa at the fracture strain of 9% while PSS-PVA exhibited a tensile strength of 9.00 MPa and a fracture strain of 32% under the same conditions. This implies that PSS-PVA@CDs possesses more superior resistance to mechanical deformation (Fig. S7). Consequently, PSS-PVA@CDs not only is endowed with remarkable stability in electrical output while bending (Fig. S8), but can also restore its electrical output performance faster than PSS-PVA under pressing (Fig. S9).

Moreover, PSS-PVA@CDs is able to be integrated into wearable objects for power generation from various moisture sources including human breath and sweat evaporation. As illustrated in Fig. 1e and f, a commercial mask equipped with the device of PSS-PVA@CDs produced an output Voc of 0.80 V (RH = ∼5% and 32 °C) after usual breathing for 15 min, while putting PSS-PVA@CDs on human arm also enabled an output Voc of 0.70 V after 10 min under the environment conditions of RH = ∼60% and 26 °C. This suggests the great potential application of PSS-PVA@CDs with high flexibility and the outstanding power output in wearable electric powering systems.

The electrical output performance of PSS-PVA@CDs was compared with other reported MEG devices in terms of the Voc and Isc as depicted in Fig. 2 a and Table S1 [25–38]. By contrast, our presented moist-electric generator has demonstrated the performance advantages of voltage and current output. As reported in literature, most of MEG devices based on various functional materials such as polymers, graphene oxides, and nanofiber fabrics enabled an output Voc of lower than 0.50 V and an Isc of below 3.00 μA for single device units. Note that although the voltage output could be larger than 1 V for some reported MEG devices, the current output was still smaller than 3.00 μA. As thus, the membrane of PSS-PVA@CDs had a better trade-off between maximum areal output power and internal resistance, facilitating a high working output.

From a practical application view, thereby we conducted proper connection to enlarge the voltage and current output. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the current output of 9.60 μA is acquired by connecting two units of PSS-PVA@CDs in parallel and about 14.2 μA can be realized as three units are connected. Meanwhile, the voltage outputs of 1.56 and 2.15 V are achieved by connecting two and three units in series (Fig. 2c and d), respectively. This electric output is sufficient for running some electronic devices (Fig. 2e), e.g. an 8-digit electronic calculator that is able to do various arithmetic operations. More importantly, PSS-PVA@CDs exhibits superior stability in electrical output and mechanical performance. The long-term stability and reproducibility of electric generation using PSS-PVA@CDs was evaluated by keeping the sample in dry condition for each cycle, overall lasting for 20 days. We observed no obvious voltage output performance decay (Fig. 2f). Meanwhile, the total materials cost for single MEG units is merely 0.27¥ based our cost accounting (Table S2), implying that our presented CDs-incorporated membrane is economic for practical implementation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The roles of CDs in MEG
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the roles of CDs in remarkable improvement performance of the PSS-PVA@CDs, we further conducted a control experiment. Taking 0.10 mL mixture of the formic acid and H2O2 in absence of CDs was added into aqueous solution of PSS and PVA to prepare a MEG membrane (PSS-PVA@FA) for comparison. The induced voltage of PSS-PVA@FA varies with the amount of formic acid and H2O2 mixture added. The output voltage displayed ascending then descending changes with its increase (Fig. S10). The induced optimal voltage was even close to that of PSS-PVA@CDs. Nevertheless, PSS-PVA@FA outputted a much lower current signal than PSS-PVA@CDs (Fig. 3 a). Unlike with PSS-PVA@CDs, moreover, its tensile strength was almost no increase compared with pure PSS-PVA (Fig. S11), implying the limited influence of formic acid and H2O2 mixture on the molecular structure of PSS-PVA.

To determine the proton migration abilities in the membrane, we measured electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the PSS-PVA, PSS-PVA@CDs and PSS-PVA@FA after exposure to moisture with the RH of 90% for 15 min (Fig. 3b). As shown in the right of Fig. 3b, PSS-PVA@CDs presents a smaller semicircular diameter of Nyquist curve than the PSS-PVA and PSS-PVA@FA, suggesting its lower moisture-induced proton transfer resistance.

Naturally, the surface of the PSS-PVA is endowed with a super hydrophilicity due to the large number of hydrophilic groups, such as –OH and -SO3H groups (Fig. S12). After introducing CDs with abundant O-related groups and/or formic acid into it, more hydrophilic groups can be involved in the membrane (Figs. S13 and S14) and lead to more superior hygroscopic performance. For instance, the XPS high-resolution spectra of C 1s and O 1s revealed the presence of new peaks assigned to carboxylic acid groups due to the incorporation of CDs (Fig. S14). From Fig. 3c, the water contact angle of PSS-PVA@CDs was examined to be smaller than that of PSS-PVA and PSS-PVA@FA, confirming its better hydrophilicity as well. Fig. 3d depicts the moisture adsorption processes of three sample membranes with time under RH of 90%. It is clear that the absorbed water content of the PSS-PVA@CDs is the maximum among three samples and the time to reach the saturation of water content is shorter than that of the PSS-PVA and PSS-PVA@FA. Accordingly, the water transport rate in the PSS-PVA@CDs is the fastest among three samples (Fig. 3e), signifying the more amount of free hydrated ions per unit time passing through per unit cross section, that is, the larger current output. Fig. 3f shows the dependence of voltage output on the time reacting with moisture. The PSS-PVA@CDs gives rise to the remarkably higher increment rate of MEG than PSS-PVA at initial stage. This verifies its more superior water adsorption and faster water transport ability as revealed in Fig. 3c-e since the MEG process is directly related with the proton concentration gradient induced by water molecule distribution in the membrane.

The CDs-enhanced electricity-generating mechanism of PSS-PVA was proposed as follows. Although no new chemical bonds are formed in PSS-PVA after incorporating CDs and/or formic acid (Figs. S13 and S14), the hydrogen-bond network could be denser owing to the introduction of new hydrophilic groups (–COOH and –OH) with CDs. Zeta potential of the aqueous solution of PSS and PVA presents great electronegativity (Fig. 4 a). After adding the suspension of as-prepared CDs, its electronegativity is enhanced further, indicating that CDs improve the stability of the PSS and PVA solution [39–41]. Fig. 4b provided the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the aqueous solution of PSS and PVA before and after adding the suspension of as-prepared CDs as well as formic acid and H2O2 mixture. It is clear that the fluorescence emission of the aqueous solution of PSS and PVA can be merely quenched by CDs’ suspension. In addition, the luminescence decays of the aqueous solution of PSS and PVA were deconvoluted with the multicomponent decay function to yield an average lifetime about 3.34 ns, while its lifetime was prolonged to 11.98 ns after adding CDs (Fig. S15). This means that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer from the PSS to CDs took place since PVA can’t emit light. According to these results and the corresponded discussion, it can be deduced that CDs selectively interact with PSS molecules through analogous intermolecular forces. As illustrated in Fig. 4c and d, CDs could induce ‘‘bridging interactions” between PSS chains after membrane formation. This result not only remarkably enhanced mechanical properties of as-prepared membrane by impeding crack propagation with CDs, but also offered holes at their formed interfaces [24].

As moisture gradually enters into the membrane, the H2O molecules will dissociate the membrane to release moveable hydrated ions from the hydrophilic groups grafted on the polymer in absence of CDs. After that, the H2O molecules are able to increasingly penetrate and wet the MEG membrane, enabling the released hydrated ions to be transferred deeply into the inner under the drive of concentration differences [17,18,42]. As thus, the electric output can be detected in the external circuit accompanying with the transport of positively charged ions and leaving the negatively charged molecular chains (Fig. 4e). In the presence of CDs, the denser hydrogen-bond network favors the quick entrance of moisture to the membrane and promotes its diffusion among polymer chains (Fig. 4f). Concurrently, the hydrophilic groups can be ionized by more moisture to release more free hydrated ions. In this case, the built-in concentration gradient of free hydrated ions could be formed faster and larger. Furthermore, the directional transport of free hydrated ions from the high concentration region to the low concentration region becomes easier owing to the presence of holes/spaces surrounding CDs. In combination of the above contributions of CDs, PSS-PVA@CDs allows it output a higher current under the same moisture conditions compared with PSS-PVA and PSS-PVA@FA.

To confirm above assumption, we firstly examined the effect of moisture adsorption on the voltage output. When the electrode upon sample membrane was not manufactured with holes, the voltage generated was near zero (Fig. 5 a), revealing that power output could be associated with the presence of water molecules in the membrane. For comparison, Fig. 5b presented the evolutionary process of voltage signals produced by PSS-PVA@CDs before and after sealing the holes on the electrode. As the PSS-PVA@CDs was exposed to an environment of 90% RH, the voltage output gradually increased and reached the maximum in 15 min, while it started to rapidly decrease after the holes on the electrode was fully sealed. We then drilled holes on both electrodes and observed a very small voltage as well (Fig. 5c). What’s more, the interfacial impedance of sample membranes became smaller with moisture adsorption (Fig. 5d). All of these results demonstrate that not only a water content gradient but also directional water diffusion inside the membrane are indispensable for power generation.

Just as aforementioned, the introduction of CDs with numerous hydrophilic groups caused the water molecules to faster penetrate and wet the membrane and could provide a high gradient of hydrated ions, accelerating the transfer of hydrated ions from the inner to the surface. To identify the number of free hydrated ions transferred to surface, the different sample membranes with same sizes and masses were immersed in the mixed solution of 6.00 mL isopropanol (IPA) and 1.00 mL water (Fig. S16). After reacting for 5 min, the mixed solution exhibited different pH values for PSS-PVA, PSS-PVA@CDs and PSS-PVA@FA respectively, which was caused by the different numbers of free protons being dissociated from membranes and their migration rates from one side to the other side. As shown in Fig. 5e, the highest H+ concentration was observed in the mixed solution containing PSS-PVA@CDs. This result proves that PSS-PVA@CDs enables more proton production per unit time compared other two samples.

According to the theory of H+ ion concentration difference cell, the maximum power output (E) can be expressed as [36],
(1)
E =
RT
F
ln
a 2
a 1
in which R is the gas constant, T is the temperature, F is the Faraday’s constant; a2 and a 1 are the activity of the H+ ions in two sides, respectively. The power output thereby highly depends on temperature and the concentration difference of hydrated ions induced by the moisture. As shown in Fig. 5f, increasing the temperature does not obviously increase the power output at above 30 °C, different from the results reported previously [43]. This implies that increasing the temperature goes against the amplification of the concentration difference of hydrated ions at the direction of vertical electrodes. On the contrary, this directional concentration difference could be reduced since the diffusion coefficient could be larger at the higher temperature [44]. In consideration of such case, the improved power output from the PSS-PVA@CDs could be mainly derived from CDs-induced bridging interactions as aforementioned.

We have demonstrated that the CDs produced by selective oxidation etching of coal pitch with of formic acid and hydrogen peroxide can be used to modify the polyelectrolyte membrane of PSS and PVA for highly efficient MEG. The centimetre-sized membrane reliably generates a sustained voltage output of 0.83 V with a short-circuit current of 5.20 μA under proper moist conditions, outperforming the power generation performance of the pristine PSS-PVA and many current MEG devices. Our experimental results reveal that the denser hydrogen-bond network could be formed in the membrane through the introduction of CDs and then facilitates water adsorption and the generation of a higher proton concentration gradient. Besides, the CDs induce bridging interactions between PSS chains and pave a fast directional transport pathway for free hydrated ions, resulting in a remarkable power output. On the other hand, the CDs can also endow the polymer membrane with more superior mechanical properties, in favor of processing and scale integration for higher power output. Accordingly, this work provides a facile and economic method for remoulding traditional polyelectrolyte membrane to realize highly efficient moist-electric energy conversion for practical applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for financial support Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province of China (20210302123037), Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2022-136) and Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.143443.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Energy harvesting by vitrimer-based moist-electric generators

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Feng, W. Zhao, Z. Yang, Y. Deng, T. Yang, Y. Ni, Energy harvesting by vitrimer-based moist-electric generators, J. Mater. Chem. A 10 (2022) 11524-11534. https://doi.org/10.1039/d1ta11029b.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-powered wearable electronics based on moisture enabled electricity generation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transparent, self-healing, arbitrary tailorable moist-electric film generator

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Moisture adsorption-desorption full cycle power generation

Bin, Sodium alginate based skin-core fibers with profoundly enhanced moisture-electric generation performance and their multifunctionality, J. Mater. Chem. A 11 (2023) 3616-3624. https://doi.org/10.1039/d2ta09432k.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance, highly stretchable, flexible moist-electric generators via molecular engineering of hydrogels
